Store credentials as build arguments passed via the CodeBuild StartBuild API — runtime arguments are not logged to CloudWatch
explain: CodeBuild natively integrates with AWS Secrets Manager and Systems Manager Parameter Store for sensitive environment variables. When the env section of buildspec.yml uses type: SECRETS_MANAGER or type: PARAMETER_STORE, CodeBuild resolves the secret at build time and automatically masks any occurrence of the secret value in build logs with '****'. Storing credentials directly as PLAINTEXT environment variables — even if encrypted at rest — will expose them in CloudWatch Logs output. Base64 encoding is not masking. KMS-encrypting logs protects against unauthorized log access but does not prevent the plain text from being written to the log stream.

q: You need to generate unique identifiers that are sortable by time. What should you use?
options: Timestamp + random number
UUIDv4
Sequential numeric IDs
UUID v1 (time-based)
explain: UUIDv1 combines timestamp with uniqueness, providing sortable unique identifiers.

q: A Lambda function has been running for weeks and suddenly stops writing logs to CloudWatch. The function is still being invoked (confirmed via Lambda metrics showing successful invocations) and there are no Lambda errors. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Lambda function code was updated to remove all console.log statements — silent execution produces no log output
CloudWatch Logs has a hard quota of 1 million log events per Lambda function per month — the function has exceeded this quota and further logging is silently throttled

The CloudWatch Log Group for the Lambda function was deleted — Lambda attempts to write logs but silently fails because the log group no longer exists and the IAM role lacks logs:CreateLogGroup permission
The Lambda function is now executing in under 1 millisecond — logs for sub-millisecond executions are automatically suppressed by the Lambda runtime
explain: CloudFormation Conditions allow conditional resource creation based on parameter values or environment context. Define conditions in the Conditions section using intrinsic functions (Fn::Equals, Fn::If), then reference them in resource properties with Condition: MyCondition. Use this to create resources only in specific environments (e.g., create WAF only in production).

q: Your Cognito user pool shows failed login attempts from a specific IP. You need to block this IP from further attempts. What should you configure?
options: Lambda trigger for custom block logic
Security group (does not apply to Cognito)
Cognito password policy
WAF rule for the Cognito domain
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy is purpose-built for Lambda-to-RDS connectivity. It maintains a pool of database connections and multiplexes thousands of Lambda invocations onto the pool. This eliminates 'Too many connections' without changing application code. RDS Proxy also provides improved failover times and enforces IAM-based database authentication.

q: X-Ray sampling is set to 1% of requests. Production experiences an outage but only 1% of traces are captured. How can this be improved?
options: Sampling rate is sufficient
Disable sampling entirely
Use sampling rules to increase rate for errors/exceptions or sample 100% of errors
X-Ray cannot sample selectively
explain: Sampling rules allow higher rates for specific paths/status codes; always sample errors (100%) for complete failure visibility.

q: An EventBridge rule triggers a Lambda function when specific S3 events occur. The Lambda processes the events but a subset of invocations fail with a transient error. The failed events are not being retried and are lost. What must be configured to ensure failed events are retried and preserved?
options: Configure an EventBridge retry policy on the rule target with retryAttempts > 0 and a maximumEventAgeInSeconds — EventBridge will retry failed Lambda invocations up to the specified number of times
Add a Lambda destination for on-failure events — Lambda destinations capture post-execution results but do not retry the original invocation
Increase the Lambda function timeout — EventBridge interprets any non-immediate response as a failure and retries only if the function times out rather than returning an error
Enable Lambda function versioning and point the EventBridge rule to a specific version ARN — versioned functions have automatic retry logic built into the Lambda service
explain: Lambda execution roles need xray:PutTraceSegments and xray:PutTelemetryRecords permissions to send trace data to X-Ray. Without these permissions, Lambda silently fails to write traces — the function runs normally but no traces appear in the X-Ray console. Add the AWSXRayDaemonWriteAccess managed policy to the Lambda execution role.

q: A Lambda function processes messages from an SQS Standard queue. After deployment, users report that some orders are being processed twice, resulting in duplicate database entries. The Lambda function is idempotent. What is the MOST LIKELY infrastructure cause?
options: Lambda reserved concurrency is set too high, causing multiple concurrent Lambda instances to poll the same SQS message simultaneously

The SQS VisibilityTimeout is shorter than the Lambda function's maximum execution time — if Lambda does not finish before the timeout expires, SQS makes the message visible again and another Lambda instance processes it
The Lambda event source mapping BatchSize is set to 1 — single-message batches are automatically retried by SQS if the Lambda response is not received within 100ms
The SQS queue does not have a dead-letter queue configured — without a DLQ, SQS redelivers all messages indefinitely regardless of Lambda completion status
explain: SQS VisibilityTimeout controls how long a message remains invisible to other consumers after being received. If Lambda begins processing a message but the VisibilityTimeout expires before execution completes, SQS assumes the message was not processed and makes it visible again — triggering a second invocation. The fix is to set VisibilityTimeout to at least 6× the Lambda function timeout (AWS recommendation). Missing DLQs affect what happens after maxReceiveCount is exceeded, not during normal processing. Lambda never polls the same message concurrently with multiple instances from the same event source mapping.

q: A Step Functions state machine fails with a 'States.TaskFailed' error and the cause message contains 'States.Permissions'. The state machine's Task state is configured to invoke a Lambda function using the Lambda:invoke action. The Lambda function exists and works when invoked directly. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The Lambda function's resource-based policy does not list states.amazonaws.com as an allowed principal, which is required even when the execution role has invoke permissions
The state machine is in a different AWS region than the Lambda function, and cross-region Lambda invocations require an explicit opt-in in the Step Functions console
The Step Functions state machine execution role lacks the lambda:InvokeFunction permission for the target Lambda function's ARN — add the permission to the execution role's IAM policy
The Lambda function's reserved concurrency is set to 0, preventing Step Functions from invoking it even though direct invocations from the console succeed
explain: A 'States.Permissions' error means the Step Functions execution role does not have permission to perform the requested API action. For Lambda invocations, the execution role needs the lambda:InvokeFunction action on the specific Lambda function ARN (or a wildcard). This is the most common cause of this error. The Lambda resource policy is not required when the calling IAM role already has the necessary IAM permissions — resource policies are evaluated only when the principal is from a different account. Zero reserved concurrency would cause a throttle error, not a permissions error. Step Functions supports cross-region Lambda invocations without a special opt-in.

q: An API Gateway REST API returns HTTP 429 TooManyRequests errors under moderate load. The API has a usage plan with 10,000 requests/day and 100 requests/second burst. The client load test confirms requests are well below these limits. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: HTTP 429 errors from API Gateway always indicate a WAF rate-based rule is blocking the client IP — check WAF logs for matching rules
The usage plan quota is shared across all API keys — if other API key holders consume quota, the new client's requests are rejected even under their own limit
Lambda integration timeout of 29 seconds causes API Gateway to return 429 when the backend is slow, rather than the standard 504 Gateway Timeout
API Gateway account-level throttling limits (default 10,000 RPS burst, 5,000 RPS steady) are being hit by the cumulative load of all APIs in the account — not just this specific API
explain: API Gateway enforces throttling at multiple levels: per-client usage plan limits, per-method throttling, per-stage throttling, and account-level limits. The account-level default burst limit (10,000 requests) and steady-state rate limit (5,000 RPS) are shared across ALL APIs and stages in the AWS account in a region. If other APIs or high-traffic stages consume this shared account quota, an individual API can receive 429 errors even when it is well within its own usage plan limits. The fix is to request a quota increase via AWS Support for the account-level limits or to reduce consumption from other APIs.

q: Your CloudFormation template needs conditional resource creation. How should you do this?
options: Conditions are not supported
Conditions section in template
Create multiple templates
Hardcode logic in Lambda
explain: CloudFormation Conditions enable conditional resource creation based on parameters.

q: Your RDS database connection from Lambda works occasionally but often fails with "too many connections" error. You increased max_connections but still fails. What should you do?
options: Use RDS Proxy to pool connections
Increase RDS instance size
Create more database replicas
Move to DynamoDB
explain: RDS Proxy maintains a connection pool and provides connections to Lambda functions, preventing connection exhaustion from many concurrent Lambda invocations.

q: A CloudFormation stack fails during creation and rolls back. The CloudFormation Events tab shows 'Resource creation cancelled' for most resources and a single vague 'CREATE_FAILED' event. A developer needs to find the specific error that triggered the rollback. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE approach?
options: Check the CloudFormation Drift Detection report — it shows configuration differences between the template and the actual provisioned resources
Increase the CloudFormation stack timeout to 3 hours — timeout-related truncation causes the vague error message
Enable CloudFormation StackSets and deploy to a second region — cross-region deployments produce more verbose error messages
Re-run the CloudFormation deployment with --disable-rollback flag — this leaves failed resources in place so the developer can inspect their configuration directly
explain: DynamoDB TransactionConflictException occurs when two concurrent transactions attempt to modify the same item simultaneously. DynamoDB uses optimistic locking for transactions — only one can succeed. Implement exponential backoff retry logic for this exception. For high-contention items, consider atomic UpdateItem operations (ADD/SET) which don't use transactions.

q: X-Ray tracing is enabled on your Lambda function but you do not see traces in the console. What is the most common cause?
options: Lambda is in a VPC
X-Ray is not enabled in API Gateway
Lambda execution role lacks x-ray:PutTraceSegments permission
X-Ray daemon is not running
explain: Lambda needs IAM permissions to write trace segments to X-Ray. Without proper permissions, traces will not be sent even if X-Ray is enabled.

q: An Amazon ECS task on Fargate fails to start and shows a 'STOPPED' status immediately after launch with no application logs visible in CloudWatch Logs. The task definition specifies a container with 512 MB memory. The ECS console shows 'Essential container in task exited' as the stopped reason. Where should the engineer look to diagnose the root cause?
options: Check the ECS service's auto-scaling activity log in the AWS console to see if a scale-in event terminated the task before it could start
Inspect the ECS cluster's capacity provider utilization to verify that Fargate spot capacity was available at the time of the launch attempt
Increase the task's memory to 2048 MB and redeploy — Fargate tasks with insufficient memory often fail silently without writing logs before the OOM killer terminates the process

Check the ECS task's stopped reason detail in the ECS console (Tasks tab → select the stopped task → Stopped reason) and review the container exit code; then check CloudWatch Logs for the log group associated with the task definition to find any output written before the crash
explain: When an ECS Fargate task stops immediately, the first diagnostic steps are: (1) check the stopped task's detail page in the ECS console — the 'Stopped reason' and per-container 'Exit code' fields reveal whether it was an OOM kill (exit code 137), a crash (non-zero exit code), or a missing image. (2) Check CloudWatch Logs for the task's log group — even a crashing container often writes error output before dying. These two sources together identify whether the issue is a misconfigured entry point, missing file, bad environment variable, or OOM. Auto-scaling scale-in events would show in service events, not as a stopped task with 'Essential container exited'. Arbitrarily increasing memory treats symptoms before understanding the cause. Capacity provider utilization affects whether the task was placed, not why it crashed after starting.

q: A CodeDeploy deployment to EC2 instances reports 'Succeeded' in the CodeDeploy console, but after checking the running application, the team confirms it is still serving responses from the old version. No rollback was triggered. What should the team investigate to understand why the new version is not running?
options: Check the Application Load Balancer's access logs to confirm that traffic was correctly rerouted to the new target group after the deployment
Verify that the CodeDeploy agent on the EC2 instances was restarted before the deployment, as a stale agent process can report success while running old lifecycle hooks
Inspect CloudTrail logs for the deployment to confirm the CodeDeploy service role had permission to write the new artifact to the EC2 instance's deployment directory

Check whether the ApplicationStart lifecycle hook in the AppSpec file correctly stops the old process and starts the new application binary; if the hook exits 0 without actually restarting the service, CodeDeploy considers the deployment successful even if the old process is still running
explain: CodeDeploy considers a deployment successful when all lifecycle hook scripts exit with code 0 — it does not verify that the application is actually running the new code. If the ApplicationStart hook is a script that exits 0 without stopping the old process (e.g., the service restart command silently fails or the wrong service name is used), CodeDeploy reports success while the old version continues running. The investigation should tail the deployment logs on the instance (/opt/codedeploy-agent/deployment-root/deployment-logs/) and manually test the ApplicationStart script. A stale CodeDeploy agent would likely cause hook timeouts, not silent success. CloudTrail permissions issues would fail the deployment, not silently deploy the wrong version. ALB routing should be checked after confirming the application itself is wrong.

q: An SQS dead-letter queue is accumulating messages. The primary queue consumer Lambda function is healthy. A developer needs to understand why specific messages are failing without re-processing them in production. What is the BEST approach?
options: Use the SQS console or CLI to receive DLQ messages, inspect the message body and system attributes (ApproximateReceiveCount, SentTimestamp), and check corresponding Lambda CloudWatch Logs for the matching message ID
Delete the DLQ and recreate the primary queue — messages that reappear after recreation are structurally invalid and can be discarded
Move all DLQ messages back to the primary queue using SQS message redrive — the consumer will reprocess them and log errors to CloudWatch
Enable SQS Server-Side Encryption on the DLQ — unencrypted messages in transit are being corrupted, causing consumer failures
explain: The correct debugging flow for SQS DLQ messages is: (1) receive the message from the DLQ to read its body and attributes — ApproximateReceiveCount shows how many times it was attempted, SentTimestamp shows when it entered the primary queue; (2) use the MessageId or a correlation ID in the body to search CloudWatch Logs for the Lambda invocation that processed it; (3) read the error in the Lambda logs to understand why it failed. This non-destructive approach lets you diagnose without affecting production. Message redrive re-processes messages, which risks re-triggering the same failures. DLQ issues are almost never caused by encryption.

q: A developer configures a custom domain name on API Gateway and maps it to a regional API. The custom domain shows 'Certificate Mismatch' in the console. The ACM certificate was successfully issued 10 minutes ago. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The ACM certificate renewal is still in progress — newly issued certificates take up to 24 hours to propagate to all API Gateway endpoints
The ACM certificate must use RSA-4096 key algorithm — RSA-2048 certificates are not supported for API Gateway custom domains
API Gateway regional endpoints require ACM certificates provisioned in the same AWS region as the API — if the certificate was created in a different region it cannot be attached to the custom domain
Custom domain names require a CloudFront distribution to be created first — API Gateway cannot directly attach an ACM certificate without an associated CloudFront distribution
explain: API Gateway enforces a strict region-certificate pairing rule: Edge-optimized APIs require the ACM certificate in us-east-1 (regardless of where the API is deployed), while Regional APIs require the ACM certificate in the same region as the API. A 'Certificate Mismatch' error typically occurs when a certificate issued in us-east-1 is attached to a Regional API in us-west-2 (or vice versa). The fix is to issue a new ACM certificate in the correct region for the endpoint type. RSA-2048 is fully supported. Regional APIs do not require CloudFront. ACM certificate propagation is near-instant once issued.

q: A Lambda function that previously retrieved secrets from Secrets Manager starts failing with 'AccessDenied' errors. No IAM policy changes were made to the Lambda execution role. The secret ARN has not changed. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Secrets Manager resource-based policy on the secret was modified to add an explicit Deny for the Lambda execution role ARN, overriding the role's identity-based allow policy
The AWS region for Secrets Manager was changed in the Lambda environment variable, and the new region requires a separate VPC endpoint
Lambda functions lose Secrets Manager access when their execution role has been used for more than 90 days without rotation — AWS enforces credential age limits
The Secrets Manager secret was rotated and the new version requires a different IAM permission (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue is now version-specific)
explain: AWS IAM evaluation logic applies an explicit Deny before any Allow. A resource-based policy on a Secrets Manager secret can contain an explicit Deny statement that overrides the Lambda execution role's identity-based allow policy — even if the role policy explicitly grants secretsmanager:GetSecretValue. This commonly happens when a security team tightens secret access policies, accidentally adds a wildcard Deny, or modifies condition keys (e.g., aws:SourceVpc). The fix is to inspect the Secrets Manager secret's resource policy for explicit Deny statements affecting the Lambda role ARN. IAM permissions on the role are not the issue since they were confirmed unchanged.

q: Your DynamoDB table occasionally shows "TransactionConflictException" when using TransactWriteItems. What does this mean?
options: Multiple transactions trying to modify same item
Encryption key issue
Write capacity exceeded
DynamoDB is unreachable
explain: TransactionConflictException occurs when concurrent transactions try to modify the same item. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff.

q: A developer's Lambda function processes orders from an SQS queue. The function fails on 10% of messages due to malformed order data. Failed messages return to the queue and are retried indefinitely, causing the same bad messages to block processing. Which configuration prevents bad messages from blocking the queue?
options: Configure a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) on the SQS event source mapping — after the maxReceiveCount is reached (e.g., 3 attempts), failed messages are automatically moved to the DLQ for inspection, while valid messages continue processing
Set the Lambda function's reserved concurrency to 1 so only one message is processed at a time, preventing bad messages from overwhelming the function
Wrap the entire Lambda handler in a try-catch block that catches all exceptions and returns success — this prevents SQS from seeing failures and removes messages from the queue
Enable SQS long polling on the queue to reduce the frequency of failed message retrieval and give the Lambda function more time between retries
explain: A Dead Letter Queue is the purpose-built solution for poison pill messages in SQS. The event source mapping maxReceiveCount defines how many times a message can fail before being sent to the DLQ. After maxReceiveCount failures, the message moves to the DLQ (not back to the main queue) — stopping the infinite retry cycle. Dead-lettered messages can be inspected, fixed, and reprocessed. Reserved concurrency=1 would serialize processing but doesn't remove bad messages. Long polling reduces idle API calls, not message retry behavior. Catching all exceptions and returning success would silently drop bad orders — data loss, not a solution.

q: A Lambda function processes large payloads (20MB JSON) from S3. The function is hitting the 6MB synchronous invocation payload limit when called directly from API Gateway. What is the MOST appropriate architectural fix?
options: Change the workflow to an asynchronous pattern: API Gateway returns a job ID immediately, the client uploads the payload to S3 directly (using a pre-signed URL), S3 event triggers the Lambda function asynchronously with the S3 object reference instead of the payload
Use API Gateway binary support with chunked transfer encoding to split the 20MB payload into 6MB chunks sent sequentially to Lambda
Increase the Lambda payload limit by requesting a service quota increase for larger synchronous payloads
Switch from REST API to HTTP API in API Gateway — HTTP APIs support larger payload sizes up to 20MB
explain: Lambda's 6MB synchronous request payload limit cannot be increased (it's a hard limit). The architectural solution is to separate the payload upload from the processing trigger. The client uploads the large file directly to S3 (no Lambda involved), and Lambda receives only the S3 object reference (S3 key), which is well under any size limit. This pattern also improves scalability (S3 handles large file uploads natively) and cost (no Lambda billable time during upload). There is no service quota increase for Lambda payload size. API Gateway chunked transfer doesn't work with Lambda's invocation model. HTTP API supports up to 10MB (not 20MB) payload — still insufficient.

q: A developer builds a REST API using Amazon API Gateway backed by Lambda. Some API endpoints return the same response for any given request (e.g., GET /products/{id}). Each Lambda invocation queries DynamoDB, taking 50-100ms. How can the developer reduce latency and DynamoDB costs for these read-heavy endpoints?
options: Enable API Gateway caching on the endpoint with a TTL of 300 seconds — API Gateway caches responses by cache key (including path parameters), returning cached responses for subsequent identical requests without invoking Lambda or querying DynamoDB
Enable Lambda Provisioned Concurrency to eliminate cold starts — warm Lambda functions respond faster, reducing the DynamoDB query time
Add ElastiCache (Redis) within the Lambda function to cache DynamoDB query results in-memory — repeated invocations return cached results
Increase the Lambda function's memory allocation from 128MB to 1024MB — more memory also provides more CPU, reducing DynamoDB query execution time
explain: API Gateway response caching stores the full HTTP response for a configured TTL. Subsequent requests with the same cache key (path parameters, headers) return the cached response directly from API Gateway — Lambda is never invoked and DynamoDB is never queried. This provides the maximum latency reduction (<5ms cached vs 50-100ms live) and eliminates DynamoDB costs for cached requests. Memory increase reduces computation time but not I/O-bound DynamoDB latency meaningfully. Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold starts (relevant when the first invocation is slow) but doesn't help for warm repeat invocations. ElastiCache in Lambda is a valid alternative but requires Lambda invocation (Lambda still runs), ElastiCache cluster cost, and network calls.

q: A developer is writing a Lambda function in Python that processes an SQS batch of 10 messages. Some messages in the batch may fail processing while others succeed. The developer wants only failed messages to be retried, not the entire batch. Which implementation achieves this?
options: Process each message in a separate Lambda invocation by setting BatchSize=1 on the event source mapping — each message succeeds or fails independently

Use the ReportBatchItemFailures feature — return a response with 'batchItemFailures' listing only the messageId values of failed messages; Lambda will only return failed messages to the SQS queue for retry while deleting successful messages
Catch exceptions for each message and continue processing; at the end, manually call sqs.delete_message() for successful messages — SQS will automatically retry messages not explicitly deleted
Set the Lambda function's concurrency to 10 to process each message in the batch concurrently, ensuring failures in one thread don't affect other threads
explain: Lambda's ReportBatchItemFailures (Partial Batch Response) feature allows returning exactly which messages failed. The response format includes 'batchItemFailures': [{'itemIdentifier': 'messageId'}] for each failed message. Lambda then: (1) Deletes successful messages from SQS. (2) Returns only failed messages to the queue for retry. Without this, if Lambda throws an exception for any reason, the entire batch is returned to the queue. BatchSize=1 solves isolation but multiplies Lambda invocations by 10 (higher cost, lower throughput). Manual SQS deletion works but requires managing deletion for each message individually. Lambda concurrency doesn't affect how one Lambda invocation processes its batch.

q: A startup uses AWS CodePipeline with CodeBuild and CodeDeploy to deploy a Node.js application to EC2. After a deployment, users report that some requests return old responses and some return new responses — indicating multiple versions are running simultaneously. The team needs all users to hit only the new version after deployment completes. Which CodeDeploy deployment configuration addresses this?
options: Use CodeDeploy Linear50PercentEvery10Minutes — this ensures 50% of instances always run the new version, preventing mixed-version responses
Use CodeDeploy Blue/Green deployment — launch a new set of instances with the new version, shift traffic to the new fleet only after health checks pass, then terminate the old fleet

Use CodeDeploy AllAtOnce deployment configuration — deploy to all instances simultaneously, ensuring all instances switch to the new version at the same time (accepts brief downtime during the deployment window)
Use CodeDeploy Canary10Percent5Minutes — deploy to 10% of instances first, wait 5 minutes, then roll out to the remaining 90%
explain: Blue/Green deployment solves the mixed-version problem: a completely new fleet (green) is deployed with the new version while the old fleet (blue) continues serving traffic. Only after health checks confirm the green fleet is healthy does the load balancer shift ALL traffic from blue to green — at which point all users hit only the new version. The old fleet is kept available for rollback. AllAtOnce deploys to all instances simultaneously — there's a period during deployment where some instances are being updated and others are serving old code, which causes the same mixed-version problem. Linear and Canary configurations intentionally run mixed versions for their ramp period.

q: A developer wants to use AWS CloudFormation to deploy the same application to dev, staging, and production environments with different configurations (instance sizes, replica counts, feature flags). Which CloudFormation feature handles environment-specific configuration MOST efficiently?
options: Use CloudFormation Mappings with an 'Environment' key to look up all configuration values based on the environment parameter
Use CloudFormation Conditions to create conditional resources — e.g., only create larger instances if 'Environment' equals 'production'
Create three separate CloudFormation templates — one per environment — and maintain each independently with the environment-specific configurations hardcoded

Use CloudFormation parameters with separate parameter files (dev-params.json, staging-params.json, prod-params.json) — the same template is deployed with different parameter files per environment using the --parameter-overrides flag in the CLI or separate parameter files in CloudFormation stacks
explain: The parameter files approach maintains a single template (DRY principle) with environment-specific values separated into JSON/YAML parameter files. The same template is deployed with --parameter-overrides pointing to the environment's parameter file. This is the most maintainable pattern: one template to update when infrastructure changes, with environment differences expressed in data (parameters), not code. Separate templates per environment cause configuration drift — changes must be made in three places. Mappings work well for lookup tables but become unwieldy for many configuration values. Conditions are best for resource existence (create/don't create), not for configuration values like sizes and counts.

q: A developer builds a public API using Amazon API Gateway that receives requests from mobile apps. The API must allow mobile apps from different platforms (iOS, Android, web) to make cross-origin requests. The OPTIONS preflight requests must return the correct CORS headers. Which is the SIMPLEST way to enable CORS for all API endpoints?
options: Enable CORS in the API Gateway console for each resource — API Gateway automatically creates the OPTIONS method with correct Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, and Access-Control-Allow-Methods headers
Configure CORS in the mobile app SDK to bypass browser CORS restrictions for API Gateway endpoints
Enable API Gateway's built-in CORS proxy feature in the stage settings, which globally adds CORS headers to all responses
Add a Lambda function to handle all OPTIONS preflight requests that manually returns the CORS headers in the response
explain: API Gateway has a built-in CORS configuration feature in the console (Actions → Enable CORS or in the resource settings for REST APIs; CORS configuration in HTTP APIs). This automatically creates the OPTIONS method for preflight requests and adds the Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, and Access-Control-Allow-Methods response headers. For REST APIs, it also adds the necessary method response headers. This is the simplest path — no custom Lambda for OPTIONS, no SDK configuration. A custom Lambda OPTIONS handler works but requires additional development and maintenance. CORS is a browser security feature that cannot be bypassed in the mobile SDK. There is no global CORS proxy in API Gateway stage settings.

q: A developer deploys a microservices application with 5 services communicating via Amazon SQS. Users report intermittent high latency. The developer needs to identify which service is causing the latency and trace requests through the entire system. Which AWS service provides this distributed tracing capability?
options: Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights — query log groups from all 5 services with a correlation ID, aggregate latency metrics across services to identify the bottleneck

AWS X-Ray — instrument each service with the X-Ray SDK (which automatically traces SQS message handling), view the service map in X-Ray console to see end-to-end latency, and drill into traces to identify which service segment is slow
Enable Amazon GuardDuty — GuardDuty detects anomalous API call patterns that correlate with performance issues across microservices
AWS CloudTrail — enable CloudTrail for all SQS API calls, query CloudTrail logs to correlate message send and receive timestamps across services
explain: AWS X-Ray is designed for distributed tracing across microservices. The X-Ray SDK automatically propagates trace context (trace ID) through SQS message attributes, allowing a request to be traced from the first service through all 5 services. The X-Ray service map visualizes each service and its latency percentiles, immediately showing which service is the bottleneck. Trace drill-downs show subsegment timing (DB calls, external calls) within each service. CloudWatch Logs Insights requires manual correlation ID implementation across all services and doesn't visualize the service topology. CloudTrail logs API calls but doesn't show internal service latency. GuardDuty is for security threat detection, not performance analysis.

q: A developer builds a web application using Amazon Cognito User Pools for authentication. After login, the user's JWT tokens (ID token, access token) are stored in the browser. The developer wants to call a private API Gateway endpoint using the user's identity. Which token should be included in the API Gateway request and how?
options: No token is needed — configure API Gateway to use Cognito identity federation, which automatically attaches the user's Cognito session to all API requests without header-based authentication
Include the username and password in each API request header; configure API Gateway with a Lambda authorizer that calls Cognito to authenticate the user on every request

Include the ID token or access token in the Authorization header of the API request; configure API Gateway with a Cognito User Pool authorizer that validates the JWT signature and expiry against the Cognito User Pool — the authorizer rejects requests with invalid or expired tokens
Use the refresh token in the Authorization header — refresh tokens have longer validity and are more secure than access tokens for API calls
explain: The correct pattern: (1) After Cognito login, store the access token and ID token (JWTs) in the browser. (2) Include the ID token or access token in the HTTP Authorization header for each API request. (3) API Gateway's Cognito User Pool authorizer validates the JWT (signature, expiry, issuer, audience) against the Cognito User Pool — no Lambda code needed. The ID token contains user claims (email, groups); the access token grants access to Cognito APIs. Both can be used with API Gateway authorizers depending on configuration. Sending credentials on every request is insecure. Refresh tokens are long-lived sensitive tokens that should never be sent to APIs — they're only used to get new access tokens from Cognito. Cognito doesn't automatically attach sessions to API requests.

q: A developer deploys a Lambda function that reads from DynamoDB and writes to S3. The function works in dev but fails in production with 'AccessDeniedException: User: arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/lambda-prod-role/function is not authorized to perform: s3:PutObject'. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The production S3 bucket is in a different region than the Lambda function — configure the S3 client with the correct region endpoint
The Lambda function has reached the S3 concurrent request limit — implement exponential backoff and retry on PutObject calls
The S3 bucket's Block Public Access settings are preventing the Lambda function from writing to the bucket

The Lambda function's execution role in production ('lambda-prod-role') is missing the s3:PutObject permission for the production S3 bucket — the dev role has this permission but the prod role doesn't. Add an IAM policy granting s3:PutObject on the production bucket ARN to the prod Lambda execution role
explain: AccessDeniedException from an assumed IAM role clearly indicates an IAM permissions problem, not an S3 or network issue. The error message names the specific role ('lambda-prod-role') and the exact action denied (s3:PutObject). This is a common environment difference: the dev Lambda role was granted S3 write permissions but the prod role was not. The fix is to attach an IAM policy granting s3:PutObject (and optionally s3:GetObject) on the specific production S3 bucket ARN to the prod execution role. S3 Block Public Access blocks public access, not IAM-authenticated role access. S3 concurrent request limits don't result in AccessDeniedException. Cross-region S3 access is supported (with correct endpoint configuration) but the error is about authorization, not connectivity.

q: A developer notices that their Lambda function occasionally takes 3-5 seconds on the first invocation after a period of inactivity, but subsequent calls are consistently fast (50ms). This causes intermittent latency spikes reported by users. What is this phenomenon and what is the MOST appropriate solution for production?
options: This is a Lambda warm-up issue — add a CloudWatch Events rule to ping the Lambda every 5 minutes, which keeps one instance warm and prevents cold starts
This is a code initialization problem — move all import statements and database connections into the handler function instead of global scope to reduce initialization time
This is a VPC attachment latency — move the Lambda function out of the VPC, which eliminates the ENI attachment time that causes cold start delays

This is a Lambda cold start — the function container must be initialized (download code, start runtime, run init code) before the first invocation. Use Provisioned Concurrency to keep pre-initialized containers ready, eliminating cold start latency for production workloads
explain: Lambda cold starts occur when a new container must be provisioned, the runtime initialized, and the handler code loaded for the first invocation of an idle function. Provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes a specified number of Lambda instances, keeping them ready with fully initialized runtimes — cold start time is eliminated for these instances. This is the production-grade solution for latency-sensitive applications. Periodic pings (warming) keep one instance warm but don't scale to concurrent invocations and only warm one container — a burst of concurrent requests still causes cold starts on new containers. Removing from VPC helps (VPC ENI creation is a major cold start contributor) but the question doesn't mention VPC. Moving imports into the handler increases per-invocation latency (runs every time) — the opposite of what's needed.

q: A developer designs a DynamoDB table for a multi-tenant SaaS application. Each tenant has thousands of users. The access pattern is: 'get all users for a tenant' and 'get a specific user by email within a tenant'. Which primary key design BEST supports both access patterns?
options: Partition key: tenant_id, Sort key: user_email — this composite key enables: (1) Query by tenant_id alone to get all tenant users (DynamoDB Query on PK), (2) Query by tenant_id + user_email to get a specific user (Query with exact SK value) — both access patterns with O(1) to O(n) complexity
Partition key: user_id (UUID), Sort key: tenant_id — use a Global Secondary Index on tenant_id to enable tenant queries
Partition key: user_email (globally unique), Sort key: tenant_id — this enables user lookup by email across all tenants but requires a full table scan to get all users for a tenant
Partition key: user_email, no sort key — create a separate DynamoDB table per tenant, using cross-table queries for multi-tenant operations
explain: The composite key (tenant_id PK + user_email SK) supports both access patterns natively: (1) DynamoDB Query with tenant_id as the partition key retrieves all users for a tenant in sorted order by email — efficient O(n) for the tenant. (2) DynamoDB GetItem with both tenant_id and user_email retrieves the specific user in O(1). This is the standard multi-tenant DynamoDB design pattern — tenant isolation through partition key. Option B (user_email PK) enables fast per-email lookup but requires a full table scan or secondary index for tenant queries. Option C requires an additional GSI and adds design complexity. Separate tables per tenant causes operational overhead and doesn't scale to many tenants.

q: A developer needs to allow a third-party application to upload files directly to an Amazon S3 bucket without exposing AWS credentials. The uploaded files must be constrained to a specific prefix, file type (image/jpeg only), and maximum size (5MB). Which approach enforces these constraints?
options: Generate a pre-signed POST URL with conditions (key prefix restriction, content-type = image/jpeg, content-length-range = 0 to 5242880 bytes) — the application uses this signed URL to upload directly to S3, with S3 enforcing all conditions without AWS credentials
Use an API Gateway endpoint that the third-party calls, which triggers a Lambda function that validates the file and then uploads to S3 — this keeps AWS credentials server-side
Give the third-party application an IAM user access key with s3:PutObject permission scoped to the prefix — IAM policies can enforce file type restrictions
Create a public S3 bucket for uploads, configure CORS, and have the third-party upload directly — use a Lambda trigger to validate files after upload and delete non-compliant ones
explain: S3 pre-signed POST is specifically designed for browser/client direct-to-S3 uploads with server-side policy enforcement. The developer generates a pre-signed POST with conditions: key must start with the allowed prefix, Content-Type must equal image/jpeg, and content-length-range constrains file size. S3 enforces these conditions on the upload — uploads violating conditions are rejected with a 403 error. No AWS credentials are exposed (the pre-signed URL encodes the signature). A public bucket accepts anything — reactive deletion after upload creates a window where bad files exist. IAM policies can restrict prefix but cannot enforce Content-Type on PutObject. API Gateway + Lambda works but introduces latency, cost, and a server to maintain.

q: A development team uses AWS CodeDeploy for EC2 deployments. After a deployment, the CodeDeploy agent reports the deployment succeeded, but the application isn't running correctly — health check endpoints return 500 errors. However, CodeDeploy marked the deployment as successful. What is the MOST LIKELY issue and fix?
options: Enable CodeDeploy deployment health monitoring in the console — this feature automatically tests application health after deployment

The appspec.yml lifecycle hooks don't include a ValidateService hook that runs health check tests against the deployed application — add a ValidateService hook script that queries the health check endpoint and fails (exit code 1) if it returns non-200, causing CodeDeploy to mark the deployment as failed and rollback
Configure the EC2 instance's Application Load Balancer health checks to check more frequently — ALB will mark the instance as unhealthy and deregister it automatically
The CodeDeploy agent needs to be updated — old agent versions don't detect runtime errors after deployment
explain: CodeDeploy lifecycle hooks define what CodeDeploy checks during deployment. Without a ValidateService hook, CodeDeploy only verifies that the deployment scripts ran without errors — it doesn't test the application's runtime functionality. Adding a ValidateService hook script that queries the health check endpoint and exits with code 1 on failure causes CodeDeploy to mark the deployment failed and trigger automatic rollback. This is exactly the gap between 'deployment scripts ran successfully' and 'application works correctly.' The CodeDeploy agent age doesn't affect health detection capability. ALB health checks deregister unhealthy instances but don't trigger CodeDeploy rollback. There is no automatic application health monitoring toggle in CodeDeploy.

q: A developer is using Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as a session store for their web application. Sessions are stored with a 30-minute TTL. During high traffic, the cache hit rate drops to 60% and some users report being logged out unexpectedly. What is the MOST LIKELY cause and solution?
options: The 30-minute TTL is too short — increase it to 24 hours to keep sessions in cache longer and improve hit rates

Memory pressure is causing Redis to evict sessions before their TTL expires — the cache is full and Redis is using an eviction policy (like allkeys-lru) that removes least-recently-used keys including active sessions. Increase the ElastiCache node size to accommodate more sessions, or switch the eviction policy to 'noeviction' and handle OOM errors in the application
ElastiCache for Redis doesn't support TTL-based session expiry — switch to Memcached which natively supports expiration
The application is creating multiple Redis connections per request — enable connection pooling to reduce connection overhead and improve cache efficiency
explain: A cache hit rate dropping from 100% to 60% combined with premature session invalidation strongly indicates memory eviction. When Redis reaches its maxmemory limit, it evicts keys based on the configured eviction policy. 'allkeys-lru' evicts least recently-used keys regardless of TTL — including sessions with 28 minutes remaining. Solutions: (1) Increase cache memory. (2) Use 'volatile-lru' eviction (only evict keys with TTL set, which is all sessions — better than evicting other keys). (3) Consider 'noeviction' which rejects new writes but preserves existing sessions (client-side OOM handling needed). A longer TTL increases memory usage (worsens the problem). Redis supports TTL natively. Connection pooling improves throughput but doesn't affect eviction.

q: A developer's Lambda function writes to Amazon DynamoDB. Under load, the function fails with 'ProvisionedThroughputExceededException'. The table has 100 WCUs. CloudWatch shows throughput is only at 80% when the errors occur. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The DynamoDB table needs to be switched from provisioned to on-demand mode — on-demand scales automatically without throughput limits

Hot partition problem — all writes are hitting the same DynamoDB partition due to a poorly distributed partition key (e.g., using a timestamp or sequential ID as PK), causing that partition to exceed its per-partition throughput limit while other partitions are underutilized. Design a more distributed partition key or add a random suffix to spread writes across partitions
DynamoDB's 100 WCU limit is being exceeded — request a WCU limit increase via Service Quotas
The Lambda function needs exponential backoff — without retry logic, DynamoDB throttles requests that arrive too quickly
explain: The key clue is 'throughput at 80% but errors occur' — if the table was simply over its total WCU limit, throttling would start at 100% utilization. Throttling at 80% indicates a hot partition: one partition key value receives disproportionate traffic. DynamoDB partitions each have their own throughput limit (~3,000 WCU for reads, 1,000 WCU for writes per partition). A hot partition hits its per-partition limit while the table's total WCU appears underutilized. Common causes: sequential IDs, timestamps, or status fields as partition keys (low cardinality). Fix: high-cardinality, evenly distributed partition keys. Exponential backoff reduces error impact but doesn't fix the hot partition. WCU limit isn't being exceeded table-wide. On-demand mode still has per-partition limits and throttles hot partitions too.

q: A developer stores API keys for third-party services in AWS Secrets Manager. The Lambda function that uses these keys retrieves them on every invocation, adding 50ms latency. The developer wants to reduce this latency without sacrificing security. Which approach reduces secret retrieval latency?
options: Store the API keys in Lambda environment variables encrypted with KMS — environment variables are loaded at container initialization, eliminating per-invocation Secrets Manager calls
Switch from Secrets Manager to AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store (SecureString) — Parameter Store retrieval is faster due to lower overhead than Secrets Manager

Use the AWS Secrets Manager Lambda extension — it provides a local HTTP endpoint that caches secrets in-process with a configurable TTL (default 300 seconds). Lambda retrieves secrets from the local extension cache instead of calling Secrets Manager directly, reducing latency from 50ms to <1ms while refreshing secrets before TTL expiry
Cache the secret in a global variable in the Lambda handler — the first invocation loads the secret, subsequent invocations in the same container use the cached value
explain: The Secrets Manager Lambda extension runs as a sidecar process in the Lambda execution environment. It caches secrets locally and serves them via a local HTTP endpoint (localhost:2773). The extension refreshes the cache before TTL expiry to pick up secret rotations. Lambda retrieves from the local cache in <1ms instead of making a network call to Secrets Manager (50ms). This achieves both goals: performance (local cache) and security (auto-refreshes on rotation). Environment variables work but don't support automatic rotation — new secrets require function redeployment. Global variable caching is what the extension manages (it's effectively the same pattern but without automatic refresh on rotation). Parameter Store has similar latency to Secrets Manager for network calls.

q: A developer uses AWS CloudFormation to deploy infrastructure. After deleting a stack, they discover that the Amazon RDS database instance was also deleted, causing data loss. How should the developer prevent this from happening in future stack deletions?
options: Move the RDS resource to a separate CloudFormation stack — deleting the application stack won't affect resources in a different stack
Add an RDS automated backup configuration in the template — automated backups allow data restoration even if the RDS instance is deleted during stack deletion

Add a DeletionPolicy: Retain on the RDS resource in the CloudFormation template — when the stack is deleted, CloudFormation retains the RDS instance and its data instead of deleting it; the resource remains available and can be imported into another stack
Enable CloudFormation stack termination protection — this prevents the stack from being deleted without first disabling termination protection
explain: DeletionPolicy: Retain is the direct solution for preventing resource deletion during stack deletion. When CloudFormation deletes a stack, resources with DeletionPolicy: Retain are skipped — they remain intact. The retained RDS instance and all its data persist after the stack deletion. The resource is 'orphaned' from CloudFormation but can be re-imported into another stack later using CloudFormation import resource functionality. Termination protection prevents accidental stack deletion but an authorized deletion still destroys the database. Moving to a separate stack is a valid architectural approach but doesn't protect against mistakenly deleting the database stack. Automated backups allow recovery but there's a backup window gap and restore takes time — prevention (Retain) is better than recovery.

q: A developer implements an order processing system where Lambda functions process orders from an SQS queue. During high traffic, orders must be processed in the order they were received (FIFO). The system processes 2,000 orders per second. Which SQS configuration meets these requirements?
options: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams instead of SQS — Kinesis preserves message order within a shard and scales to 2,000 records/second per shard
Use an SQS FIFO queue with a single message group — a single message group guarantees strict ordering for all messages across the entire queue at 300 TPS

Use an SQS FIFO queue with message group IDs — orders with the same message group ID are processed in strict FIFO order. For 2,000 TPS, use multiple message groups or request a quota increase for FIFO queue TPS (default is 3,000 TPS with batching or 300 TPS without batching per message group)
Use a standard SQS queue with a sequential message ID in each message body — the Lambda function sorts messages by ID before processing to enforce order
explain: SQS FIFO queues guarantee strict ordering per message group ID. For 2,000 TPS: a single message group provides 300 TPS (or 3,000 TPS with batching). Using multiple message group IDs (e.g., order category or customer segment) provides horizontal scaling while maintaining order within each group. Standard SQS doesn't guarantee ordering — client-side sorting is unreliable. A single FIFO message group is limited to 300 TPS — insufficient for 2,000 TPS. Kinesis is a valid alternative for ordered streaming but SQS FIFO is the direct answer for the SQS requirement and the question asks about SQS configuration.

q: A developer is designing a DynamoDB-backed application that needs to support two access patterns: (1) get an order by order_id (frequent), and (2) get all orders for a customer sorted by date (frequent). The primary key is order_id. How should pattern 2 be supported?
options: Store a customer_orders list in a separate DynamoDB item with customer_id as the key, maintaining a sorted list of order IDs — retrieve the list then batch-get each order
Use a DynamoDB Scan with a FilterExpression on customer_id and sort the results in the application layer after retrieval
Add a Local Secondary Index (LSI) on order_date — LSI allows sorting within the same partition key (order_id), enabling date-based queries per order

Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) with customer_id as partition key and order_date as sort key — queries against the GSI with customer_id retrieve all orders for that customer sorted by order_date without scanning the full table
explain: A Global Secondary Index with customer_id as PK and order_date as SK provides exactly the access pattern needed: Query the GSI with customer_id to retrieve all orders, returned in order_date sort order. This is O(n) where n is the customer's order count — no table scan needed. DynamoDB Scan reads the entire table (O(all items)) and is extremely inefficient for selective queries. An LSI uses the table's partition key (order_id) as its PK — LSI can only add a different sort key within the same partition, which doesn't help query by customer_id. The separate list item approach is a valid pattern (adjacency list) but requires multiple operations and has list size limits (400KB item limit).

q: A Lambda function integrated with API Gateway returns HTTP 502 Bad Gateway errors intermittently. CloudWatch Logs show the Lambda function completed successfully (no errors in logs). What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Lambda function is timing out — 502 errors from API Gateway always indicate a Lambda timeout, even if CloudWatch shows completion
The Lambda function's VPC configuration doesn't allow outbound HTTPS to API Gateway — add an outbound rule for HTTPS to the API Gateway endpoint IP range
API Gateway has a 10-second timeout limit — if Lambda takes more than 10 seconds, API Gateway returns 502 regardless of Lambda completion

The Lambda function is returning a response body that API Gateway cannot parse — API Gateway Lambda proxy integration requires a specific response format with 'statusCode', 'headers', and 'body' fields. If Lambda returns a non-conforming response (e.g., returning a Python dict directly instead of the proxy integration format), API Gateway returns 502
explain: In API Gateway Lambda proxy integration, the Lambda function MUST return a specific JSON structure: {statusCode: 200, headers: {...}, body: 'string'}. If Lambda returns a different format (e.g., {'message': 'success'} without statusCode) or returns an incorrectly formatted body, API Gateway cannot parse the response and returns 502. This is invisible in Lambda logs because Lambda completed successfully — the error is in API Gateway's response parsing. The Lambda logs would be clean. Lambda timeout causes 504 (not 502). API Gateway's maximum timeout is 29 seconds. Lambda in a VPC communicates with API Gateway via its service endpoint — the Lambda function doesn't initiate a connection to API Gateway (API Gateway calls Lambda).

q: A developer processes records from Amazon Kinesis Data Streams using an AWS Lambda event source mapping. The Lambda function averages 200ms per record. The stream has 5 shards and the Lambda event source mapping has a batch size of 10. Monitoring shows the consumer is falling behind the producers. Which configuration change MOST increases throughput?
options: Enable Lambda's parallelization factor (set to 5-10 per shard) in the event source mapping — this allows multiple Lambda invocations per shard to process different batches concurrently, multiplying throughput by the parallelization factor
Increase the number of Kinesis shards from 5 to 10 — more shards allow more Lambda invocations in parallel
Increase the batch size from 10 to 100 — processing 10x more records per Lambda invocation improves throughput
Reduce the Lambda function's memory from 512MB to 128MB — lower memory means faster invocations and higher throughput
explain: By default, Lambda processes one batch per shard at a time. With 5 shards and 200ms per record × 10 records = 2 seconds per batch, throughput is 5 shards × 10 records / 2 seconds = 25 records/second maximum. The parallelization factor allows multiple concurrent Lambda invocations per shard (up to 10). With parallelization=10 and 5 shards: 50 concurrent Lambda invocations × 10 records / 2 seconds = 250 records/second — 10x improvement. Increasing batch size to 100 increases throughput (100 records / 2 seconds = 5x) but also increases end-to-end latency per record. Adding more shards helps but is more expensive than enabling parallelization. Reducing memory reduces CPU available — likely increases per-record processing time (worse throughput).

q: A developer uses AWS CloudFormation to deploy infrastructure. They need the Lambda function's ARN to be available as a parameter in another CloudFormation stack (Stack B) that deploys the API Gateway using that Lambda function. Which CloudFormation feature enables cross-stack parameter sharing?
options: Use CloudFormation Outputs with Export in Stack A to export the Lambda ARN, then use Fn::ImportValue in Stack B to reference the exported value — this creates a cross-stack dependency where Stack A must be deployed before Stack B
Use CloudFormation Nested Stacks — package Stack B inside Stack A as a nested stack, passing the Lambda ARN as a nested stack parameter directly
Hardcode the Lambda ARN in Stack B's template — retrieve the ARN from the Stack A console after deployment
Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to share the Lambda ARN between stacks — Stack A writes the ARN to SSM, Stack B reads it using Fn::ImportValue
explain: CloudFormation Exports/Imports are the native cross-stack reference mechanism: (1) Stack A adds an Outputs section exporting the Lambda ARN with a unique export name (Export: {Name: !Sub '${AWS::StackName}-LambdaArn'}). (2) Stack B uses Fn::ImportValue to reference the exported name. This creates an explicit dependency — CloudFormation prevents Stack A from being deleted while Stack B is importing its values. Nested stacks embed one stack in another — they're part of the same stack hierarchy, not independent stacks. Hardcoding is error-prone and breaks automation. SSM Parameter Store is a valid alternative (Stack A writes to SSM, Stack B uses ssm: dynamic references) — but Outputs/Exports is the direct CloudFormation-native answer.

q: A developer builds a mobile app that needs to allow unauthenticated users to read public content from an Amazon S3 bucket and authenticated users to write to a specific S3 prefix. The authentication uses Amazon Cognito. Which AWS service allows the developer to assign AWS credentials to both authenticated and unauthenticated users with different permissions?
options: AWS IAM with SAML federation — configure SAML identity provider integration with Cognito and issue different IAM roles based on the SAML assertion's authentication status attribute
Amazon Cognito User Pools — configure User Pool groups with different IAM roles attached; unauthenticated users get the default guest group role

Amazon Cognito Identity Pools (Federated Identities) — configure an Identity Pool with an unauthenticated IAM role (read-only S3 permissions) and an authenticated IAM role (read + write to specific S3 prefix). The Identity Pool issues temporary AWS credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity based on authentication status
Amazon API Gateway with Cognito authorizer — all S3 requests go through API Gateway, which checks Cognito tokens and routes to different Lambda functions based on authentication status
explain: Amazon Cognito Identity Pools specifically provides the mechanism for issuing AWS credentials to both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) mobile users. The Identity Pool has two IAM role configurations: (1) Unauthenticated role — permissions for anonymous users (S3 read-only). (2) Authenticated role — permissions for Cognito User Pool authenticated users (S3 read + write to /authenticated-users/ prefix). The app calls the Cognito Identity Pool to get STS temporary credentials, which are used directly to call AWS services. Cognito User Pools handles user authentication but doesn't issue AWS credentials. SAML federation is for federated enterprise identities, not mobile app users. API Gateway + Lambda proxying adds latency and complexity when direct S3 access with credentials is sufficient.
